titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Alfred Mitchell Bingham and the Common Sense Collection
Bingham (Alfred Mitchell) and the Common Sense Collection
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Saturday October 24, 2020 at 08:06

did.unittitle: Alfred Mitchell Bingham and the Common Sense collection
did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence, writings, printed material, and other papers of Alfred Bingham, social reformer, writer, founder and editor of Common Sense, lawyer, and politician. Included are his personal papers, consisting of diaries, writings and correspondence, much of the latter being with individuals and organizations prominent in the reform movements of the 1930s, 1940s, and 1960s. Also included are the business and correspondence files of Common Sense, and files relating to various organizations with which he was associated. Correspondents of note include Paul Douglas, Charles Beard, Chester Bowles, Lewis Corey, John Dewey, Theodore Dreiser, Aldous and Julian Huxley, Henry Pratt Fairchild, Charles Merriam, John Haynes Holmes, Anne Lindbergh, Alexander Meiklejohn, Eleanor Roosevelt, Bertrand Russell, and Norman Thomas.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright has been transferred to Yale University for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Alfred Mitchell Bingham and the Common Sense Collection (MS 148). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Alfred Bingham in 1952 and 1979.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in eight series and one addition: I. Correspondence. II. Subject Files. III. Writings. IV. Newspapers. V. Special Files. VI. Common Sense Correspondence Files. VII. Common Sense Special Files. VIII. Scrapbook.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Alfred Bingham was born in 1905, the third son of Hiram Bingham III and Alfreda Mitchell Bingham. He graduated from Yale College in 1927 and Yale Law School in 1930. After obtaining his law degree, he embarked on a two year trip around the world, visiting several countries and meeting and interviewing many international figures for American newspapers. Upon his return, he began the progressive journal Common Sense with Selden Rodman, which the two of them owned and operated until it ceased circulation in 1945. In the 1930s, Bingham was also involved in a variety of progressive organizations and movements, including the League for Independent Political Action. In 1940, Bingham was elected on the Democratic ticket to the Connecticut State Senate in which he served one two year term. From 1944 to 1946, Bingham served in the United States Army in Germany. After World War II ended, he settled in Southeastern Connecticut where he practiced law and became involved in many local, state, and national organizations which worked for social reform in a variety of areas. Bingham is the author of many articles and books on subjects including political theory, economics, democracy in Germany and the Soviet Union, and the Bingham family in Connecticut.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of correspondence, writings, printed material, and other papers of Alfred Bingham, social reformer, writer, founder and editor of Common Sense, lawyer, and politician. Included are his personal papers, consisting of diaries, writings and correspondence, much of the latter being with individuals and organizations prominent in the reform movements of the 1930s, 1940s, and 1960s. Also included are the business and correspondence files of Common Sense, and files relating to various organizations with which he was associated. Correspondents of note include Paul Douglas, Charles Beard, Chester Bowles, Lewis Corey, John Dewey, Theodore Dreiser, Aldous and Julian Huxley, Henry Pratt Fairchild, Charles Merriam, John Haynes Holmes, Anne Lindbergh, Alexander Meiklejohn, Eleanor Roosevelt, Bertrand Russell, and Norman Thomas.

The Alfred Bingham Papers consist of the personal papers of Alfred Mitchell Bingham and the files of Common Sense, the magazine Bingham edited from 1932 until 1946 when it was absorbed by the American Mercury.

Alfred Bingham was born in Cambridge, Mass., in 1905. His great-grandfather and grandfather, Hiram Bingham I and Hiram Bingham II, were linguists and missionaries in Hawaii. Alfred Bingham's father, Hiram Bingham III, taught at Harvard, Princeton and Yale; took part in the Yale-Peruvian expeditions; served as Lieutenant Governor of Connecticut, 1922-1924; was elected Governor in 1924; and having served one day, was chosen U.S. Senator, which position he retained until 1933. (See the "Bingham Family Papers" and the "Bingham-Yale-Peruvian Expedition" collection in this library.)

Alfred Bingham was the third of seven brothers. He was educated at the Adirondack-Florida School, Groton, Yale College and Yale Law School. He was admitted to the Connecticut bar in 1930, but did not practise law until after the second World War. In 1934 he married Sylvia Doughty Knox. Between 1930 and 1932 Alfred Bingham travelled extensively in the Near East, India, the Far East, the Soviet Union and Europe. As a correspondent for the Hartford Times and other New England papers he interviewed Gandhi, Chiang Kai-Shek, Mussolini and other prominent figures. His experiences during these years, especially his visit to Russia where the first Five Year Plan was underway helped to shape his political views. Bingham believed that some form of social planning or "production for use" should replace the capitalist "production for profit" system, although he thought that Marxist theories did not take into account many factors present in modern society, in particular the middle classes.

In 1932, Bingham and Selden Rodman launched Common Sense, a monthly journal of progressive opinion and comment. Bingham and Rodman believed that they were following in the footsteps of Thomas Paine and took the title of his famous pamphlet for the name of their magazine. Their original platform called for a return to the ideals of 1776, and stated that "a system based on competition for private profit can no longer serve the general welfare."

In 1934 Bingham was arrested and jailed in Jersey City for picketing during a civil rights demonstration. He was later released on appeal. Between 1932 and 1936 Bingham devoted considerable time and energy to third party movements. In 1933 he became executive secretary of the Farmer Labor Political Federation, established by the League for Independent Political Action's "United Conference for Progressive Political Action". Bingham was also executive secretary for the American Commonwealth Political Federation, which suceeded the Farmer Labor Political Federation in 1935. These organizations, associated with the LaFollette Progressives in Wisconsin and the Minnesota Farmer-Labor Party, had intended to run candidates in the 1936 elections, but many sections of the movement, fearing a Republican victory, withdrew their opposition to Roosevelt. After 1936, Bingham himself supported the New Deal. In fact he eventually became skeptical of the possibility of organizing a national liberal movement, and in 1941 he ran and was elected as a Democrat to the Connecticut State Senate, where he became chairman of the Senate Agricultural Committee (1941-1942). In 1944 Bingham entered the army as a Military Government Officer and served as a labor specialist, mainly in Germany. After the war Common Sense ceased publication and Bingham began to practise law. He also served as Workmen's Compensation Commissioner from 1949 to 1951 during the administration of Chester Bowles, then Governor of Connecticut. In addition Bingham served on many boards and committees, including the Board of Directors of the American Civil Liberties Union, 1940-1947.

Alfred Bingham's published books include: Challenge to the New Deal, a symposium co-edited with Selden Rodman (1934), Insurgent America; the Revolt of the Middle Classes (1935), Man's Estate; Adventures in Economic Discovery, which is partly autobiographical (1939), The United States of Europe (1940), The Technique of Democracy (1942) and The Practice of Idealism (1944).

The Alfred Bingham papers are divided into seven series, which fall into two main groups. Series I - V consist of the personal correspondence and papers of Alfred Bingham while Series VI and VII are the files of Common Sense. The seven series are: Correspondence, Subject Files, Writings, Newspapers, Special Files, Common Sense Correspondence Files, and Common Sense Special Files.

The bulk of the material in Correspondence and Subject Files is arranged under subject headings which, in most cases, are the names of movements or organizations to which Bingham belonged, or in which he was interested. Alfred Bingham Correspondence contains both letters received by Bingham, his own retained file copies, and a small quantity of correspondence among other persons, most of which was probably passed on to Bingham for his information. Bingham corresponded with many of the politicians, liberals, and intellectuals of his day, and often wrote or received letters in his capacity as an official or member of various reform organizations. Correspondents of note include, among others: Charles Beard, Chester Bowles, Lewis Corey, John Dewey, Paul Douglas, Theodore Dreiser, Henry Pratt Fairchild, John Haynes Holmes, Anne Lindbergh, Norman Thomas and Oswald Garrison Villard. A small portion of correspondence is of a purely personal nature, but Bingham knew many of his correspondents in both official and personal capacities. Some letters are routine and pertain to the day-to-day administration of the journal, but many letters also discuss civil rights, the third party movement, and politics in general. Of particular interest is the extensive correspondence (1933-1943) with Thomas Amlie, Progressive Congressman from Wisconsin and chairman of both the Farmer Labor Political Federation and the American Commonwealth Political Federation. There is also considerable correspondence (1935-1936) with Nathan Fine, Director of the National Office of the American Common-wealth Political Federation, who worked closely with Amlie and Bingham. There is also correspondence pertaining to the case of Jerome Davis, professor in the Yale Divinity School, whose contract was terminated allegedly because of his economic and social views, and correspondence concerning the Rust Cotton Picker Company, in which Bingham was a shareholder.

The Subject Files contain such material as circular letters, minutes of meetings, press releases and reports relating to the many organizations, publications and subjects in which Alfred Bingham was interested. There is considerable material relating to the American Civil Liberties Union, the American Commonwealth Political Federation and the Farmer Labor Political Federation. Also of interest is the material relating to co-operatives, various peace movements and organizations devoted to "technocracy", including the People's League for Abundance.

The collection also contains addresses and articles by Alfred Bingham and other writers including Jerome Davis and Upton Sinclair; copies of news-papers with a progressive political orientation, many of which are first issues, and miscellaneous materials, such as copies of proposed legislative bills, lists of names, newspaper clippings and notes.

The correspondence files of Common Sense consist of letters received by the magazine and the retained file copies of replies written by various staff members, including Alfred Bingham, Sidney Hertzberg and Selden Rodman, all editors, Richard Rovere, managing editor, and Katrina McCormick, publisher. A large part of the correspondence concerns the acceptance or rejection of articles, or comments on editorials and the general content of the magazine. Many prominent politicians and intellectuals, however, also contributed to and corresponded with the editors of the magazine, including Pearl S. Buck, Stuart Chase, Lawrence Dennis, Jerome Frank, Aldous Huxley, Julian Huxley, Charles Merriam, Eleanor Roosevelt, Bertrand Russell, Upton Sinclair, Stephen Spender and Wendell Wilkie. There is also a small section of the personal correspondence of Sidney Hertzberg, and Robert G. Spivack, assistant editor of Common Sense.

The Special Files of Common Sense contain correspondence and other material related to the operation of the magazine, such as its finances, advertising, distribution and general organization. There is also material from scrapbooks, mainly newspaper clippings referring to Common Sense or containing reprints of Common Sense articles. Of particular interest is 'Beyond Defense', a section of correspondence and writings related to a series of articles about post-war planning published in Common Sense and intended for future publication in book form. Among the contributors are: A.A. Berle, Count R.N. Coudenhove-Kalergi, Charles W. Eliot, Thomas H. Eliot, Henry Pratt Fairchild, Herbert Harris, Hans Heymann, Eliot Janeway, Harold Loeb., Rexford Guy Tugwell, Jerry Voorhis and Barbara Wooton.
A full set of Common Sense is available in the general collection of the Sterling Memorial Library.
Addition Description

The May 1979, June 1979, and July 1979 additions to the Alfred Mitchell Bingham and the Common Sense Collection comprise Addition 1. The papers augment the first accession through the documentation of Bingham's personal life and correspondence, political career, monograph writings, and volunteer activities. The addition also adds other writings, professional papers, and photographs to the main accession. Although the papers range from 1905 to 1979, the bulk of the materials runs from the mid 1920s to the early 1970s. Correspondence with family and close friends and diaries, including a detailed travel journal he kept during a 1930-1932 world tour, provide documentation of Bingham's private and personal affairs. Bingham's brief career as a Connecticut state senator from 1941 to 1943 is also documented in these papers. The addition includes multiple files which reflect Bingham's professional and volunteer involvement with national, state, and regional progressive groups and organizations devoted to legal aid and lawyer referral, democracy in Germany and the Soviet Union, region and city planning, the peace movement, and public welfare, among others. A small but substantive set of files concerning the journal Common Sense also constitutes a part of the addition. Finally, there are many of Bingham's writings, including drafts for several of his books and articles, his poetry and literary prose, and his student essays.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence is arranged alphabetically by name of person, organization, or subject. Most of the subject headings are the same as those in the Subject Files. The correspondence within each group is arranged alphabetically by the name of the person with whom Bingham is corresponding, and chronologically within each division. The Series is divided into three sub-sections: "General Correspondence"; "Internal Correspondence", which consists of correspondence pertaining directly to the internal affairs of an organization; and a small section of "Correspondence of Others". Names which appear under several headings in this Series have been cross-referenced.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The SUBJECT FILE is arranged alphabetically by name of person, organization, or subject. Most of the subject headings are the same as those found in Series I. Material included under each heading is arranged alphabetically by category and chronologically within each category.

Production for use congress: newspaper clippings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This Series is divided into two subsections; "Writings of Alfred Bingham" and "Writings of others". "Writings of Alfred Bingham" is arranged alphabetically by category and alphabetically by title or subject within each category. "Writings of others" is arranged alphabetically by the name of the writer, except for a small group of reviews of Bingham's books, which are arranged alphabetically by the title of the book under review.

did.unittitle: "Industrial Expansion as an Aid to Peace"
"Is the Battle of Britain America's Battle?"
"Is there still opportunity for Youth?" (radio script)
to the National Committee of the Socialist Party [by A. Bingham?]
"Plenty for all through production for use"
"The Role of America in the World Today"
"What kind of a crisis for Democracy?"
to the Yale Socialist Club [by A. Bingham?]
"Youth in a changing America" [by A. Bingham?]
Reviews of "Insurgent America", "Man's Estate" and "The United States of Europe"

odd.p: For oversize newspapers, see boxes 33 and 34.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: COMMON SENSE CORRESPONDENCE FILES is arranged alphabetically by the name of the person or organization and chronologically under each name. At the end of the Series is a small section containing the personal correspondence of Sidney Hertzberg, editor, and Robert G. Spivack, assistant editor.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: COMMON SENSE SPECIAL FILES are arranged alphabetically by category. Within each main category material is arranged alphabetically by type, name of author or correspondent and chronologically under each heading.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Scrapbook 32a, Folios 33 and 34 are described in the main body of the register.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence is arranged alphabetically by individual or corporate body. Bingham's personal correspondence comprises the bulk of the series, and most folders contain both incoming and outgoing letters.

Correspondence with family members, particularly Bingham's parents Hiram Bingham III and Alfreda Mitchell Bingham (who became Alfreda Gregor), brothers Hiram IV, Brewster, Charles, Jonathon, and Woodbridge, and wife Sylvia Knox Bingham constitute a substantive part of this series. Letters Bingham wrote jointly to his parents are listed under "Bingham, Alfreda Mitchell and Hiram III," while letters he sent to and received from them individually are filed under the respective names. Particularly interesting are letters Bingham wrote to his parents and other family members during his world travels from 1930 to 1932. These letters include detailed descriptions and impressions of the countries be visited and provide a very useful supplement to the travel diary he kept.

The series also holds several letters Bingham wrote to and received from Yale University classmates and other close friends including Chester Bowles, John McDill, John H. G. Pierson, Selden Rodman, John C. Ross, and Pat A. Sloan. The series also includes a small amount of professional correspondence, particularly in cases where Bingham had a social relationship with the correspondent. Most of Bingham's professional correspondence in this addition can be found in the Political Files and Subject Files series. Cross-references to specific individuals with substantial amounts of correspondence found in other series are provided.

did.unittitle: Bingham family [miscellaneous correspondence with extended family members]
Bingham, Alfreda Mitchell and Hiram III [letters to both parents]

odd.head: General
odd.p: See also: box 14, folder 27 See also: box 10, folder 40 See also: box 32, folder 18

odd.head: General
odd.p: See: box 8, folder 22-24 See: box 1, folder 8-16 See: box 2, folder 17-26

did.unittitle: Pollard, Frank E. [re: AMB's children at Medomak Camp, Washington, Maine]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Personal Papers include seven sections: Bingham's "Code," diaries, family records, financial records, job inquiries, memorabilia, and scrapbooks.

Bingham's "Code" is one substantial letter he wrote to his parents in 1926 on the occasion of his twenty-first birthday, which articulated his philosophical beliefs and positions on a variety of subjects.

The "Diary of a World Tour," a travel journal Bingham kept during an almost two year journey around the world from 1930 to 1932, comprises one of the most interesting sources in this collection. In it, Bingham writes his accounts and impressions of the countries he visits, including Great Britain, Egypt, India, the Phillippines, Japan, China, Russia, Turkey, Greece, and Italy. As the son of a United States senator and acting as a reporter for the Hartford Courant and the Springfield Republican, Bingham was able to obtain brief audiences with international figures such as Mahatma Gandhi, Benito Mussolini, Alexandros Papanastasiou, Hu Shi, and other high ranking officials and prominent individuals in each country he visited. The diary contains his notes and transcriptions from several of these interviews and occasionally typescript copies of articles he wrote for the American newspapers. Additional articles can be found in the Newspaper section of the Writings series. Bingham also explicitly discusses his personal relations and sexual encounters during his travels. Finally, he recorded many of the philosophical deliberations he faced while traveling. Most notably, Bingham seriously considered becoming a Communist as a result of his travels in the Soviet Union, and his extensive deliberations on this topic can be found in the journal, part of which he unsuccessfully attempted to publish as a monograph entitled "A Bourgeois Meets Bolshevism." The typescript for this monograph can be found in the Writings series.

The family records and the financial records in this series contain correspondence, reports, and other papers documenting Bingham's work as caretaker of property and as legal counsel for family members. The financial records, excluding "Insurance policy," "General," and "Book royalties," concern properties or corporate entities Bingham jointly owned with his brothers.

Bingham's efforts to find employment in an academic setting after returning from duty in World War II comprise the bulk of the Job Inquiries section. The section also contains two folders, "Central Intelligence Agency" and "International Cooperation Administration," which reflect his attempts to join the American foreign service at the beginning of the 1960s.

Memorabilia and scrapbooks from Bingham's childhood, academic career, and travels constitute the final two sections of this series. The scrapbooks contain clippings, programs, brief letters, and other mementoes pertaining to Bingham and his family members. For further documentation of Bingham's studies at Yale University, consult the Writings series.

did.unittitle: "Code" [letter to parents giving a statement of beliefs]
1942, Sep 11 [re: decision about joining the World War II effort]
Union and New Haven Trust Company
International Cooperation Administration [re: obtaining employment in Guinea]
University of Chicago, Institute for Public Affairs
Yale University [proposal to establish a program in "Politics and Public Administration"]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Materials documenting Bingham's participation in the government and the electoral process comprise Political Files. The series is divided into three sections: Campaigns, Democratic Party, and Connecticut State Senate.

Arranged chronologically, the Campaigns section includes correspondence, clippings, speeches, flyers, and press releases documenting Bingham's attempts to get himself elected to political office. In 1940, Bingham was elected to the Connecticut State Senate, but he failed in his efforts for re-election in 1942. Bingham either seriously considered running or ran for election to the United States House of Representatives in 1952, 1966, and 1970, but he never received the Democratic Party nomination. Materials created and collected by Bingham while working for other campaigns, most notably the Eugene McCarthy presidential campaign in 1968, are also included. "Biographical information," within the 1940 Connecticut State Senate campaign, gives an excellent, though brief, description of Alfred Bingham's career up to 1940.

The Democratic Party files sporadically document Bingham's work for statewide and regional Connecticut Democratic organizations. The materials are arranged alphabetically and include correspondence, speeches, reports, and memoranda.

The Connecticut State Senate files provide documentation of Bingham's single term in the Connecticut State Senate to which he was elected in 1940 and served from 1941 into 1943. The section includes correspondence, clippings, speeches, and subject files. The subject files constitute a large sub-section and mostly pertain to broad issues or specific committees on which Bingham worked while a senator. Some of these files extend past Bingham's tenure in the senate, reflecting a continuing interest in the particular subject.

did.unittitle: 1952 United States House of Representatives, Connecticut Second District
1966 United States House of Representatives, Connecticut Second District
1968 Eugene McCarthy for President, Connecticut Committee
1970 United States House of Representatives, Connecticut Second District
1972 Connecticut Second District [AMB delegate to state Democratic party convention]
New London County Democratic Organization [re: meeting arrangements and recruitment of speakers]
Tolland County, Connecticut Democratic Organization [speech at a Democratic dinner]
Waterford, Connecticut [speech at a Democratic dinner]
Public Health and Safety, Committee on

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subject Files documents the professional, intellectual, and volunteer interests and career of Alfred Bingham. The series is arranged alphabetically by organization, geographic area, and broad topic. Although the inclusive series dates begin in 1932, the bulk of the material falls between the middle 1940s and the early 1970s. The files reflect Bingham's professional activities as an attorney and publisher, and his extensive volunteer and activist efforts in regional, state, and national movements and organizations.

Important files on the journal Common Sense, which Bingham began and edited with Selden Rodman, document the final years of the serial and its demise. These files include correspondence, internal memoranda, and other records which supplement and continue the Common Sense series in the first accession of these papers. Notable is the role that Bingham's wife Sylvia played in the administration of the journal while he was in the army in Germany from 1944 to 1946. The series contains other files relating to Bingham's publishing career, most notably in the World section, which pertains to a journal dedicated to the questions and problems posed by the world government movement. This journal was proposed and investigated but never published.

The series also includes records from local branches of national organizations to which Alfred Bingham belonged, such as the American Civil Liberties Union and Americans for Democratic Action. The series documents Bingham's instrumental role in establishing the Connecticut chapter of the ADA.

Bingham wrote extensively on the political theory of democracy and he became increasingly interested in democratic movements and possibilities in post World War II Germany and in the Khrushchev era in the Soviet Union. While in the army during and after World War II, Bingham was stationed in Germany, which gave him expertise in conditions in that country and interest in establishing a democratic government there. The files listed under "German democracy" reflect his views on the subject and his participation in several American groups dedicated to bringing about a democratic Germany. Bingham's interest in the Soviet Union began with his travels there in 1931 and was rekindled by trips there in 1959 and 1962. Files listed under "Soviet Union" document Bingham's travels and provide cross references to his extensive writings on the subject of Soviet democracy. The series also includes materials documenting Bingham's military service, listed under United States Army.

Other files in the series document Bingham's volunteer activities and political activism in the areas of legal aid and lawyer referral, regional planning, public welfare, Connecticut voter district reapportionment, and the anti-war movement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Five sections comprise Writings: articles, books, literary, newspapers, and student materials. Each section is arranged alphabetically by title. Correspondence, research files, and other papers relating to specific works are also included and are filed with their respective pieces.

Articles are predominantly typescripts for both published and unpublished essays written by Bingham on a variety of topics, including German democracy, the Soviet Union, and voter district apportionment in Connecticut. Two folders of book reviews are listed at the end of this section.

Typescripts, drafts, correspondence, outlines, and research notes constitute the Books section. Files for most of Bingham's published monographs can be found in this section. A book with the working title "Soviet Democracy" which Bingham thoroughly researched and outlined but never actually wrote is included in the files, along with two other unpublished typescripts, "A Bourgeois Meets Bolshevism" and "A Study of Communist Russia in 1931," which Bingham wrote during his world travels in the early 1930s.

Literary writings include poetry, plays, and short stories Bingham wrote in the 1920s and 1930s. Although some of these works were published in Yale literary journals, most of the pieces are unpublished.

Newspaper writings include clippings of articles Bingham wrote for the Springfield Republican and the Hartford Courant during his world travels. Typescripts for some of these articles can be found in the diaries in Series II. The small section also contains letters Bingham wrote to editors of papers on various topics.

Essays Bingham wrote for courses while attending Yale College and Yale Law School comprise the final section of this series. These writings document Bingham's early intellectual development and are good examples of the work done by a top student at Yale in the 1920s.

did.unittitle: "Can a Liberal Support the Dirksen Amendment?"
"Democracy Behind the Iron Curtain: A Preliminary

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs are arranged alphabetically and include pictures of Alfred Bingham, his family, and other relations.
